item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beautifully eerie. I get such a bittersweet, melancholy vibe from this article while at the same time being genuinely ominous. I like that it suggests more than it reveals, and the fact that the object of desire/devotion is an androgynous man just makes it better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And then you have people like me, where the gay part went over our heads but still enjoyed the relationship, and the Skip none-the-less. Stupid Crazy Pheromones By went over our heads, i mean i didn't even realize that that was a factor in which was at play here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An Scp article was not a good place to discuss A’s personality - why should the foundation care about that?

… as I stated in my further opinion:

the SCP article format doesn't naturally lend to much building of such a character

Though I disagree: there are ways of developing a richer character whilst still retaining a format worthy of suspension of disbelief. It just takes a longer word count than this.

However, I plan on writing prose about said first encounter and more in-depth exploration into August’s mindset and his obsession

Glad to hear it. I'll keep an eye out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for your further opinion. An Scp article was not a good place to discuss A’s personality - why should the foundation care about that? Our structure was to discuss the anomaly as it was needed, and not really the “why”. However, I plan on writing prose about said first encounter and more in-depth exploration into August’s mindset and his obsession, including the mentioned incident that caused the distress leading to his initial memory wipe.

Yes, we could have entered more personality and humanity into the article, but ultimately, it would not lend itself to the containment of the anomaly and thus is useless to the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice. High-quality concept, with lots of subtle details- and the execution is great. Unlike some Skips, which I have to force myself through, you had me slipping right along through this narrative- without losing clinical tone. The art was lovely, as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think I was aiming for body horror with any of the details I implemented, or with how organisms rot. They mostly serve to convey atmosphere, and going into detail would likely distract from the main thrust of the piece.

As lilDecemberist said, it would be nice to know what we could do to make this less dry and typical. Given your other comments, it seems like what you're looking for is some sort of additional anomalous effect — while I think the piece is at or near its limit for different anomalous effects as it is, I think we're both open to suggestions.

Keeping in mind, naturally, that the driving force behind this narrative is a personal encounter with some sort of unfathomable, but compelling, divine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As for the geological connection, working off the premise that -B is a God or God-adjacent, I decided to play up the idea of an underworld God by having him specifically rise up from the ground, with the disturbances heralding his arrival. The integration with the ground — beyond serving as additional weirdness and giving a convenient excuse for the Foundation to leave him there — made the subterranean connection literal.

As for what exactly is going on in-universe… well, that's not something I necessarily care to work out if I have strong imagery to work with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't have a way to describe the final image without losing the ability for readers to perceive their own idea of what happened.

Other than that, what do you think could be done to make t less "dry and typical"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and that the "gay" element didn't ruin it

We're a pretty tolerant lot. Most long-time members or even members of a few months will likely not care about who or what your SCP is- it's just a matter of how well the story is conveyed.

That said, this one went okay-ish with me (fairly mundane in all honesty- the body horror wasn't written in a sufficiently grotesque manner IMO, and the pacing felt a bit too quick. a lot more could have been done here.) up to the last log. It felt very dry and typical, and I didn't feel any strong characterisation from 3606-A. It's the 'strong and reserved' archetype that I've seen done before with far more success, so you lose a lot of punch there.

this wasn't received as "edgy"

Eeeh, not 'edgy', per say, but certainly melodramatic.
I don't understand the significance of the final image either. Maybe we're not meant to know, but I feel as though there was some wasted potential there. It doesn't end on a particularly satisfying note.

Unique, certainly eerie, but ultimately doesn't carry its own weight. -1

EDIT: I'll elaborate.

+ show
- hide

I don't think I was aiming for body horror with any of the details I implemented, or with how organisms rot.

Even if it's not what you were aiming for, I believed it had a strong presence from the start that stayed in the back of my mind for the remainder of the article- of course, that could just be me, so take that as you will.

Given your other comments, it seems like what you're looking for is some sort of additional anomalous effect

Not really. I think a more rich character from 3606-A was what I needed from this article. Which leads nicely into my next point:

Keeping in mind, naturally, that the driving force behind this narrative is a personal encounter with some sort of unfathomable, but compelling, divine.

dry and typical

There's very little emotion coming through the words- 'They swallow. In him, I am swallowed. Within him, I see' is clearly written in quite a dramatic manner, but feels wooden and stiff without anything supporting this type of character. Yes, the SCP article format doesn't naturally lend to much building of such a character, but I firmly felt as if the one interview wasn't quite enough. I would have liked to see an initial encounter, perhaps. The very early stages of contact with August. I don't like being immediately dunked into this strange mystical babble, and it does come across as quite obnoxious the more you read. It's not different. It's a very typical depiction of someone under a trance, spouting vague references and praise, and I think a subversion of that concept would have worked a little better. I think you've played it a little too safely.

'He… He sat beside me. He was looking down the hill, over the river. I said his name… His… It came out wrong, I couldn't hold it in my mouth. And he… he smiled. At me!' comes across in the same fashion- the stammering, the pronoun game, the faux mystique of it all. It's what your mind immediately goes to when you think of 'hypnotized' or 'in awe' or whatever you want to describe the state August is in as. I have to congratulate you on making a story have a pretty decent depth in such a short word count, but I don't think the brevity works to its advantage, and a lot more could have been done with regards to the character development.

Just as an aside:

I melted. I… And he… With me… We both…

Should be:

I melted. I… and he… with me… we both…

Without the capitalization. This applies to any word following an elipsis.

I don't think you should change it, because the reception here is overwhelmingly positive and you've clearly struck a chord with the majority: I just wanted to push my silly $0.02 out there in the interest of your later articles. I hope to see something in future that I can get a little more involved in.

I didn't have a way to describe the final image without losing the ability for readers to perceive their own idea of what happened.

Then I'm unfortunately able to understand it. Maybe I'm missing something, but it appears meaningless from my perspective. Sorry. My -1 stands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dark, mysterious, and unique. Excellent work, you two!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Taffeta, thank you so much for your input. This was my first attempt at creating an SCP and I'm amazed people liked it as much as they did. I hope to provide even better content in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will write more gay SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for your critique. The cowriter and I both agreed about the pronouns and I went and edited that. There is no real geological connection that I particularly thought about; those were Communism's additions and I liked them. In particular, I wanted the article to highlight the relation between the entity and a singular person. We aren't sure if everyone he contacts is drawn to subject B in this way, or if it is only subject A. But I wanted to imagine him as a brainwashed, love-addled servant, a priest to a deity he can only see when he closes his eyes.

I'm so happy I was able to convey the mystery, adoration, and overall unease I was after. I hoped that by keeping subject B mostly in the dark and providing only what the Foundation can see from without and what subject A can see from within, I could create in all of us a tiny, scant part that longs to serve as subject A does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks so much. I'm really glad you enjoyed it. I am planning to write prose connecting to it soon, but I will try to keep the overall mystique of the anomaly as it is, and contain much of the supernatural element in the dark.

item.title: Re: h
item.content:encoded: Thank you so much for your input. I'm overwhelmed with how positive the reception of my first attempt at an SCP has been, and I could not have come close without my cowriter's help. I worked hard on the interview, trying to balance pure emotion with clinical structure attempting to calm it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was nightmarish without being obvious. I liked that.
My interest was piqued by the live cow in the barn, and it just went from there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't worry; anyone who thinks an SCP is bad because it's gay is a fool and not worth taking their critique seriously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you all so much for your excellent reception. I was very passionate about this project and am delighted to have worked with Communism will Win, who ultimately took my idea and made it into an SCP in a way I realized I was unable to. He did my vision so much justice.

I'm glad that this wasn't received as "edgy" and that the "gay" element didn't ruin it - I honestly couldn't see this working out as a "heterosexual" dynamic for some reason.

I hope to provide more great work for the SCP community in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't get me wrong, this was a good scp without, but I liked that it was gay. There should be more gay scps. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3606-A: Yes. I think. Why don't the eyes come out right? I want everyone to see them.

Dr. Cabrini: I am going to look at your drawing, August. May I take it?

SCP-3606-A: Yes. Go ahead. You see? They aren't right. They aren't how they're supposed to be.

Upvoted for the most accurate description of life drawing I've read on this site, as well as a basic how-to on creating atmosphere in SCPs. Some SCPs either undershoot or overshoot the mark on providing details that add to a surreal, ominous mood, but I think this strikes a pretty good balance between 'too grounded' and 'too lolrandom'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Intriguing.

+ Show
- Hide

What I'm getting from this on the first read-through is that -B draws people to it, dominates their mind so they love him like a Cult Leader, wants them to drink of its liquid (in the cavern underneath his body, I'm guessing), and it ultimately will consume them/free them in some fashion.

I'm not sure why, but they ending made me -happy- in some weird way. It's like an anomalous cult leader who can actually give the ecstasy afterlife he promises.

I liked how you described how he forgot his family, and how he forgot those people in his dream as he was describing the dream. Nice touches.

One thing I don't understand is the geological connections.

One problem, there are a few places you refer to -A as "he" and other places you refer to -A as "it." I don't have a problem with either one (I prefer "he") but you should at least be consistent, especially sentence to sentence.

SCP-3606-A experiences regular dreams featuring an individual he refers to as "████████", whose appearance is consistent with that of SCP-3606-B; he also reports experiencing waking hallucinations consistent with these dreams on occasion. SCP-3606-A is obsessed with SCP-3606-B, spending the majority of its spare time producing artistic works that depict SCP-3606-B in some manner, though it frequently expresses dissatisfaction with its works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't even pretend to understand what the hell is going on, but it's weird and curious and creepy and I like it. +1

item.title: Re: h
item.content:encoded:
(As of Revision 2)
Little reminder, your images appear to not have been uploaded as files.
They aren't showing up on the page, but regardless I think you have made a quality SCP here, well done.
I especially like the feeling of true confusion in the interview log.
+Upvote

Edit: (As of Revision 4)
The Images are there and they are very well drawn complements to lilDecemberist.

item.title: h
item.content:encoded:
A collaboration between lilDecemberist and myself! They sketched out the plot, wrote the interview, drew the two pieces of art, and reviewed the final draft; I collated all this information and wrote the containment procedures and description, did copy-editing, and did some image editing.

The first image and the paper textures of the second two are all CC0 (public domain). Here's the source on the first one: https://pixabay.com/en/sweden-countryside-house-rural-2030918/
